rulings.Case Title: EDDIE STEWART ET AL VS. AUTO PARTS WHOLESALE ET AL
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, December 03, 2024, in Department 301, Line 1. Plaintiff's Motion for Order Granting Preference in Setting Case for Trial, and Extending Discovery Cutoff is GRANTED under C.C.P. section 36(a). 1. The trial date is March 17, 2025, at 11:15 a.m. in Department 206. a. Last day pursuant to C.C.P. section 36(f) is March 26, 2025. b. The parties shall follow the California Rules of Court, San Francisco Local Rules, and Local Rule 20. 2. The last day for hearing summary judgment/adjudication motions is March 11, 2025. a. Summary judgment/adjudication motions shall be brought on regular notice pursuant to the relevant provisions of the C.C.P., unless the parties stipulate otherwise. b. Before a party files and serves a summary judgment/adjudication motion, it must contact the clerk to make a reservation. c. The Court allows a maximum of four summary judgment/adjudication motions per day to be calendared, unless good cause is found to exceed this number. Contact the clerk to schedule a good cause hearing. 3. Time to respond to written discovery not yet served is shortened to 20 days. a. For written discovery that has already been served, responses are due within 20 days of this hearing or by the date determined by the C.C.P., whichever is earlier. b. Any issue/dispute that requires meet and confer, shall occur in person or via telephone, not by email or letter. 4. Electronic service is considered the equivalent of personal service. 5. The fact discovery cut-off date is February 28, 2025. 6. The expert discovery cut-off date is March 14, 2025. 7. All bankruptcy documents shall be turned over to defendants no later than December 26, 2024. a. If Plaintiff submits documents to bankruptcy trusts after this date, they must notify Defendants no later than five days after submission. b. The fact discovery cut-off does not apply to bankruptcy documents. 8. No later than December 10, 2024: a. Plaintiff must provide a statement to each Defendant stating the name and contact information of each lay person with knowledge of any facts supporting Plaintiff's claims as to each Defendant. b. Defendants must provide a statement stating the name and contact information of each lay person with knowledge of facts supporting Defendant's affirmative defenses. c. Any person not identified on the statements may be barred from testifying at trial or presenting a declaration on a summary judgment motion. 9. A further case management conference is set for January 28, 2025, at 4:00 p.m. a. Case management conference statements are not required. b. Parties may appear at the conference by zoom. c. If the parties agree that no CMC is necessary, they shall notify the Court at least two court days before the hearing. 10. Plaintiffs will send a settlement demand to Defendants no later than February 3, 2025. Response to the settlement demand is due February 18, 2025. The parties shall separately submit confidential settlement demands/offers. This confidential value must be separately submitted to Department503@sftc.org via electronic mail on February 25, 2025. 11. A mandatory settlement conference is set for Thursday, March 13, 2025, at 9:30 a.m. in Department 503. Personal appearance is required by all unless a party is excused for good cause. A request to excuse a personal appearance must be submitted to Department503@sftc.org two weeks before the mandatory settlement conference. = (301/RCE)

rulings.Case Title: MARY ANDERSON VS. MANNINGTON MILLS INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, December 03, 2024, in Department 301, Line 3 (Part 1 of 2). Defendant Mannington Mills, Inc's., Motion for Summary Judgment, or in the alternative, Summary Adjudication is DENIED. Opposition filed by Plaintiff. Reply filed by Defendant. The Court took judicial notice of Exhibit 1 (Plaintiff's Complaint). Plaintiff's evidentiary objections to Defendant's declarations of Bradway and Durham are sustained. Plaintiff's objections to the Bradways declaration paragraphs 4 and 9 are sustained. Plaintiff's objection to the entire Durham declaration is sustained. The Court notes several matters with respect to Defendant's separate statement. Defendant's separate statement fails to comply with CRC 31.350(d)(3) and (h). It includes a collection of multiple facts and fails to include separate citations. Moreover, Defendant's motion for Summary Adjudication violates both 437c(f)(1), and CRC 3.1350(d), (f) and (h). The supporting statement in the separate statement fails to specify the grounds upon which summary adjudication is sought. These matters notwithstanding, Defendant has not sustained its initial burden. Defendant fails to sustain its initial burden of showing Plaintiff does not possess and cannot reasonably obtain needed evidence of asbestos exposure and causation. (Aguilar v. Atl. Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 855.) The relevant issue upon consideration of Defendant's Motion is whether Defendant has produced evidence proving that "plaintiff does not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, needed evidence" to establish that Defendant's products exposed Plaintiff to asbestos. (Id. at p. 854.) Here, Defendant offers Plaintiff's deposition testimony to argue that Plaintiff had no personal knowledge of the brand name, manufacturer or supplier of any old flooring that may have been removed at Guanella Brothers or Les's Custom Floors (SUMF 6.) Specifically, Defendant argues that Plaintiff had no personal knowledge that her husband, her son, or anyone else had ever removed any flooring manufactured by Defendant at any location. (Id.) However, Defendant's argument that Plaintiff's testimony precludes the possibility of Defendant having been a source of exposure because Plaintiff did not recall the brand name, manufacturer, or supplier of flooring is not persuasive. (Continued on the next line.)

rulings.Case Title: MARY ANDERSON VS. MANNINGTON MILLS INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT MANNINGTON MILLS, INC. MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT OR, IN THE ALTERNATIVE, SUMMARY ADJUDICATION
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, December 03, 2024, in Department 301, Line 3 (Part 2 of 2) continued from previous line. Defendant Mannington Mills, Inc's., Motion for Summary Judgment, or in the alternative, Summary Adjudication is DENIED. Opposition filed by Plaintiff. Reply filed by Defendant. Plaintiff testified that when she worked at Guanella Brothers and Les's Custom Floors, she regularly cut, handled, and swept up Defendant's flooring with asbestos felt backing. (Rose Decl., Ex. A [Plaintiff's Depo, August 14, 2024] at 19:22; 20:7; 20:8-9; 20:12; 33:2-5; 37: 4-10.) A plaintiff's ignorance is by itself insufficient to demonstrate that plaintiff lacks the ability to produce evidence supporting her claim and does not serve to shift defendant's initial burden. (Weber v. John Crane, Inc. (2006) 143 Cal.App.4th 1433, 1439.) Here, Plaintiff recalled the brand names of the "new" flooring being installed, including Defendants. As such, Defendant has not made an affirmative showing that Plaintiff will be unable to support the reasonable inference that Defendant exposed Plaintiff to asbestos. In addition, Defendant states that no witnesses deposed in this case had personal knowledge that anyone ever installed or removed Defendant's flooring in Plaintiff's presence. (SUMF 10-11.) However, aside from Plaintiff there are three other witnesses in this case, Teresa Parlett, Darrel Anderson, and John Garcia, who all declared they saw Plaintiff interact with Defendants flooring with asbestos felt backing. (Rose Decl., Ex. F [Declaration of Teresa Parlett] at 13; Id., Ex. I [Declaration of Darrel Anderson] at 8; and Id., Ex. J, [Declaration of John Garcia] at 8.) Moreover, Defendant failed to depose John Garcia in this case who worked with Plaintiff. Additionally, Defendant argues that with respect to Defendant's flooring materials, Plaintiff only ever recalled linoleum. (SUMF 4.) Defendant argues that it never sold or manufactured linoleum products. (SUMF 27.) However, the allegations in this case involve vinyl sheet flooring. Plaintiff provided a declaration explaining that at her deposition she was using the term linoleum generically. (Rose Decl., Ex. D [Declaration of Plaintiff] at 9.) Witness Teresa Parlett also testified that she used the terms interchangeably during her deposition. (Rose Decl., Ex. F, [Declaration of Teresa Parlett] at 9.) While Defendant argues that the declarations contradict prior testimony, the Court finds that any discrepancies are not so stark as to render the declarations inadmissible. Instead, the declarations raise questions of credibility and factual interpretation, which are reserved for the trier of fact. Defendant fails to foreclose the possibility that Plaintiff may yet prove asbestos exposure and causation attributed to Defendant. = (301/RCE)
